audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Paul before Agrippa, Pharisee hope, persecution accountability, Damascus commission, light to nations, Festus interruption, Agrippa response.

verses.restored: Agrippa said to Paul, It is permitted for you to speak for yourself. Then Paul, stretching out the hand, began to defend himself:
Concerning all the things of which I am accused by Judeans, King Agrippa, I consider myself blessed to be about to defend myself before you today,
especially because you are knowledgeable of all customs and questions among Judeans; therefore I ask you to hear me patiently.
My manner of life from youth, having been from the beginning among my nation and in Jerusalem, all Judeans know,
having known me from the first, if they are willing to testify, that according to the strictest party of our religion, I lived as a Pharisee.
And now I stand being judged for hope in the promise made by the Divine to our fathers,
to which our twelve tribes, earnestly serving night and day, hope to arrive. Concerning this hope I am accused by Judeans, O king.
Why is it judged unbelievable among you if the Divine raises dead ones?
I myself therefore thought it necessary to do many opposing things against the name of Jesus the Nazarene.
This I also did in Jerusalem; many of the holy ones I locked in prisons, receiving authority from the chief priests, and when they were being killed, I cast my vote against them.
Punishing them often throughout all the synagogues, I tried to compel them to blaspheme; being exceedingly enraged against them, I pursued them even to cities outside.
In these things, journeying to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief priests,
at midday on the road, O king, I saw from heaven a light beyond the brightness of the sun shining around me and those journeying with me.
When we all had fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew dialect, Saul, Saul, why do you pursue me? It is hard for you to kick against goads.
I said, Who are you, Lord? The Lord said, I am Jesus, whom you pursue.
But rise and stand upon your feet; for to this I appeared to you, to appoint you as servant and witness both of what you have seen and of what I will show you,
rescuing you from the people and from the nations, to whom I send you,

to open their eyes, to turn them from darkness to light and from the authority of the adversarial one to the Divine, so that they may receive release of sins/misalignments and inheritance among those sanctified by trust in me.
Therefore, King Agrippa, I did not become disobedient to the heavenly vision,

but first to those in Damascus, and also Jerusalem, and all the region of Judea, and to the nations, I was announcing that they should turn/realign and return to the Divine, doing works worthy of turning.
Because of these things, Judeans seized me in the temple and were attempting to kill me.
Therefore, obtaining help from the Divine until this day, I stand bearing witness to small and great, saying nothing except what the prophets and Moses said was about to happen:
that the Messiah was to suffer, that being first from resurrection of the dead, he was about to announce light both to the people and to the nations.
As he was saying these things in defense, Festus says with a great voice, Paul, you are mad; much learning is turning you to madness.
But Paul says, I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I speak words of truth and sober-mindedness.
For the king knows about these things, to whom I also speak boldly; for I am persuaded none of these things is hidden from him, for this has not been done in a corner.
King Agrippa, do you trust the prophets? I know that you trust.
Agrippa said to Paul, In a little, are you persuading me to become Christian?
Paul said, I would pray to the Divine, whether in little or in much, not only you but also all those hearing me today might become such as I am, except for these bonds.
The king rose, and the governor, and Bernice, and those sitting with them.
Withdrawing, they were speaking to one another, saying, This human does nothing worthy of death or bonds.
Agrippa said to Festus, This human could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar.
